data.title: Comex Report: Ignore The Paper Price And Watch The Physical Metal
data.description:
The CME Comex is the exchange where futures are traded for gold, silver, and other commodities. The CME also allows futures buyers to turn their contracts into physical metal through delivery. You can find more detail on the CME here (e.g., vault types, major/minor months, delivery explanation, historical data, etc.).

The data below looks at contract delivery where the ownership of physical metal changes hands within CME vaults. It also shows data that details the movement of metal in and out of CME vaults. It is very possible that if there is a run on the dollar, and a flight into gold, this is the data that will show early warning signs.

Gold

Despite the fall in gold prices this month, this March saw the highest deliveries for a minor month since last May. Last May was right near the tariff uncertainty, where there were massive spreads in the price, creating an arbitrage opportunity. This had gold traveling from London to New York and pushing up vault volumes. Deliveries have been strong ever since.

The 14k contracts delivered would have also been considered quite strong when looking at any period prior to the arbitrage dislocation last year. Deliveries averaged closer to 5k prior to 2025. While the price did fall in March, some of which could be due to Turkey dumping a chunk of reserves, demand for physical metal remains quite robust.

Figure 1: Recent like-month delivery volume

Another interesting data point is the total notional delivered. The chart below shows the dollar amount of deliveries for gold specific to the month of March. While this March had delivery volumes almost 5k contracts lower than last March, from a dollar perspective it actually exceeded last March by a wide margin ($2.1B or 38%).

Figure 2: Notional Deliveries

Net new contracts (contracts that open and settle for immediate delivery) show another clue to the strength. Nearly 12k contracts were opened and delivered immediately. This made up 82% of total delivery volume. It trailed only last January and May. This means that while the paper price of metal has been under pressure all month, physical demand remained very strong.

Figure 3: Cumulative Net New Contracts

Perhaps the most important data point is the actual metal leaving Comex vaults. When metal is “delivered,” it does not mean it's leaving the Comex. It means that Registered ownership is moving from one party to another. When the metal leaves the vault, that is where things get interesting.

As shown, last year saw a major influx as the arbitrage trade took hold, but that metal has been flowing back out. In the latest month, Registered metal continued to see a move down. This metal did not flow into Eligible, which stayed relatively flat. Instead, the metal left the vault entirely. At the same time, Eligible also saw continuous and slow outflows.

This all fits the story above, a buyer throughout the month opening contracts, taking immediate delivery, and then removing it from the vaults. This is a slow, methodical buyer who is taking advantage of the price dip.

Figure 4: Inventory Data

Looking ahead to the April delivery period (a major month for gold), we see a contract that is hovering near the lower end of the average.

Figure 5: Open Interest Countdown

With the recent drop in inventory, the open interest relative to physical stocks is in trend with the recent months.

Figure 6: Open Interest Countdown Percent

Bottom line, March saw continued strength in delivery volume, a decent amount of physical metal left the vault entirely, and now the Comex has to deal with a major month which could see billions more delivered and leaving the vault. Once physical inventories get low enough, the ability for paper shorts to push the price down will be nearly gone.

Silver

While the gold market is showing a futures price that is getting higher above the spot rate (which caused metal to move from London to New York last year), the silver market is experiencing the opposite problem, backwardation. The spot price is above the futures price. This is actually a bigger warning sign because it highlights the pressure being put on the physical market.

Market Signal: Backwardation often signals a strong immediate demand or a supply shortage in the current spot market. The market is willing to pay a premium for the asset now, as the shortage is typically expected to resolve over the long term.

The backwardation ended on March 23rd, but it is dipping back down already. With the major price dislocations of silver between the East and West, it is unlikely that this type of backwardation will resolve anytime soon.

Figure 7: Spot vs Futures

Silver delivery drifted down again when compared to December and all the major months in 2025 except for July. Volume was well north of the average prior to the 2025 delivery surge.

Figure 8: Recent like-month delivery volume

Switching to notional values and focusing on the month of March produces the chart below. While most of this is price appreciation (over 7,000 more contracts were delivered in Mar 2025 vs Mar 2026), it still shows a very strong appetite for physical metal. Almost $4B of silver was delivered in this March, which is a massive volume.

Figure 9: Notional Deliveries

Similar to gold, net new contracts were a major driver, with the second-highest amount since last September.

Figure 10: Cumulative Net New Contracts

Silver-eligible inventories have absolutely plummeted. Over 70M ounces of metal have left the Eligible category in 2026 alone. At this pace, Eligible will be empty by Q4 of this year.

Figure 11: Inventory Data

Registered metal (metal available for delivery) is also seeing major outflows. Since the start of the year, 50M ounces have left the vault. That means 120M ounces, or $9B, of silver will have completely left the Comex in 2026. This has happened continuously and without pause day after day. If this trend continues at the current pace, Registered will be completely empty before September of this year.

Figure 12: Inventory Data

As we approach April delivery, the silver contract is at the lower end of the trend, but as we have seen, it will really come down to net new contracts.

Figure 13: Open Interest Countdown

On a relative basis, the open interest is actually on the higher side due to the massive drawdown in inventories.

Figure 14: Open Interest Countdown Percent

Conclusion

Every single data point above points to a singular outcome: physical metal is being bought and removed from Comex vaults on a consistent and continuous basis. This is a steady pull that is happening slowly day after day, week after week, and month after month. No panic buying, no massive moves. Just a drip, drip, drip of metal leaving the Comex. This is strategic buying with steady demand. The paper price is not having any impact. This is intentional accumulation of physical metal.

If trends continue, all the metal will be drained from Comex by the end of the year. Likely, something will happen before that occurs. They will refill coffers or start forcing cash settlement. It is getting harder to refill the coffers, though, as physical supplies remain tight around the world. If Comex starts forcing cash settlement, then it will be clear that the paper and physical markets have fully dislocated. So just remember, if you don’t hold it, you don’t own it!

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Energy Is My Favorite Sector - I'm Getting Ready To Buy It On Weakness
data.description:
Introduction

I have written a lot on energy. That may not come as a surprise to many. Last year, I covered it so much that I was worried it would bother some people. Luckily, that wasn't the case.

One of my favorite articles I have written on energy is the one titled "Energy Is, By Far, My Favorite Sector For 2026." In that article, I had to admit that I had become too bullish too soon due to risks like the supply glut. However, I also made clear why I remained bullish, which included these three reasons.

  • Major producers started to reduce output, mostly due to unfavorable prices and dwindling Tier 1 reserves in places like the Permian Basin.
  • Demand growth was likely underestimated.
  • Investors were extremely bearish.

My strategy is always focused on buying high-quality companies in unloved areas. I buy them as long as they are out of favor. Often, that results in being somewhat early. However, that's a risk I am willing to take, as I believe that nobody can time the market.

So far, that has worked out quite well in the energy space, as the sector is indeed the best place in 2026. Year-to-date, energy is up 36% while I am writing this. That's 40 points above the return of the S&P 500 and almost 30 points above the 7% return of the materials sector.

Moreover, the surge was so wild that the ratio of energy stocks to the S&P 500 (see the chart below) has skyrocketed to November 2023 levels. In other words, energy has erased more than two years of severe underperformance within the span of less than four months.

With that said, this isn't a victory lap. Although I am super glad my thesis paid off so well this year, allowing me to beat the market, I'm not done. My thesis wasn't that energy would do well for a few months. My thesis was, and still is, that energy is the place to be for years to come.

That's what this article is about.

So, let's get right to it!

Energy Crashed The Market. Ignore That Part

In January and February, energy also led the market. Back then, it was much easier for me to take credit, as the surge was based on the factors that I actually predicted. This included accelerating economic growth (i.e., ISM Manufacturing Index), short-covering of bearish energy investors, and the absence of new bearish supply headlines.

Then the Iran War happened. That's what put energy on everyone's radar, as oil prices suddenly started to dominate global headlines. This includes headlines like Australian gas stations are literally running out of gasoline and other refined fuels. From a supply chain point of view, that makes sense, as the Strait of Hormuz handles roughly a quarter of seaborne global oil supply and a fifth of liquid natural gas. Once that supply gets cut, it's basically a ripple effect through global supply chains, as prices spike and eventually even physical supplies start to dwindle. That's a major economic risk and the reason why the market has become so nervous.

Although I have said for many years that volatile geopolitics are a reason why I own defense stocks, energy, and stock exchanges that benefit from volatility, I did not predict the Iran War.

And, while my thesis looks good, I have also often said that I do not want to see sky-high energy prices. As bullish as I may be, I do not want an environment of >$90 WTI. That's based on the simple reason that economies need affordable energy. Because many oil companies are efficient, all energy bulls need is $75-$85 WTI to enjoy high cash flows without elevated economic risks that could break the consumer.

I am painting with a broad brush, but that's basically the gist of it.

Right now, I'm bringing all of this up because, despite my bullish view, my thesis isn't that you should chase momentum here. Although nobody seems to know how long this conflict could last, I know that I do not want to add to energy sector stocks in an environment where it's on everyone's mind. As most of us likely bought last year, we don't have to chase here.

No, I am writing this article to tell you that we need to closely watch energy to buy it from the people who may sell it to us once the Iran War headlines start to indicate less supply risk.

At that point, I believe the market will start to treat energy again as the "dead money" trade it was last year, likely failing to realize that major bullish shifts are happening under the surface.

Suddenly, Energy Supply Is Bullish

While I am writing this, NYMEX WTI trades in the low-$90s. By the time you are reading this, it could be $80, $120, anything in between, or something even "wilder." That's how fast headlines move.

I'm fine with that, as I don't care about short-term prices. My point is that at current prices, something very interesting is happening. As you may have expected, money managers are very bullish. CME Group (CME), which owns NYMEX, shows that money manager net long positions are at one of the highest levels in years (the green line in the chart below).

Producers, however, have started to go big into bearish investments. That's not because they are bearish but because whenever oil spikes, they tend to hedge themselves.

Especially after two tough pricing years, they can finally de-risk themselves.

That's an important fact, yet not the development that matters. What matters even more to my thesis is that oil producers are not increasing production. Technically, an environment of elevated prices, geopolitical tailwinds, and more hedges is a great opportunity to boost supply a bit.

However, according to the recently released Dallas Fed Energy Survey, that's not happening. Looking at the oil production indicator, we see that there's no change at all, as the number of companies that cut production equals the number of companies boosting output.

That's due to an increasing difference between small and large players. Roughly 70% of large E&P (exploration and production) companies reported no changes in drilling plans. If we include the fact that 80% of U.S. production is dominated by these companies, it explains why we shouldn't expect higher output.

Between 2013 (start of the shale revolution) and 2021, these were mostly focused on boosting output. In recent years, they shifted their focus to reserve protection and free cash flow growth.

This is important to keep in mind, and accelerated by two other factors. One factor is that companies have found out that the consistent rush for higher output resulted in too much supply. That hurt prices. Basically, for a full decade, energy companies spent aggressively on CapEx without creating shareholder value. That's changing now. The other reason is resource depletion. While my opinion is that the U.S. will not run out of good spots to drill, companies don't have the favorable Tier 1 resources they had a few years ago.

To give you a great example, American companies are still ultra-efficient. Existing wells are breakeven in the $40s WTI range. This means that most are printing cash right now.

However, as most production in the U.S. relies on fracking, these companies deal with high decline rates, which means new wells quickly lose momentum, which forces producers to drill new wells to maintain production.

Last year, the average price needed to drill a new well was $65 in the Permian. Now, it's $67. In other words, prices below $67 will likely result in declining output over time. Also, we clearly see the impact of inflation on rising breakevens. That's bullish for oil prices, as oil companies are basically being forced to be more prudent.

I also thought about the bearish arguments.

  • When the war in Iran ends, the risk premium will fade. That's true, without a doubt. And I honestly hope it happens. After all, that's when I will buy more energy. What the market often forgets is that the physical market isn't the market we trade every day. Even if oil prices were to slump, we're now dealing with an environment where the biggest growth engine of the past 20 years (U.S. shale) is running out of momentum. Especially when oil prices drop, that factor will become stronger.
  • Even better, after the war, all excess OPEC supply will be gone. There's no glut anymore, and prices won't be high enough to get any major global player to boost output again. In fact, I expect an Iranian de-escalation to result in a continuation of my growth acceleration thesis. That would even increase oil demand.
  • I would even make the case that supply has become so tight that a 1-2 million barrel a day (depending on who you ask) demand decline due to temporary growth headwinds can be absorbed.

That's why I am so bullish on energy and believe we should closely watch it for new opportunities the moment favorable ceasefire headlines cause oil and energy to drop.

Where I'm looking For Value

I like two (geographic) areas to invest in.

  • The American Permian Basin.
  • The Western Canadian Sedimentary Basin.

The first basin is America's biggest oil basin. While this basin is also losing steam due to dwindling Tier 1 reserves, it's the number one place where American drillers expect growth to come from, as the chart below shows.

In this area, I like high-margin producers like Diamondback Energy (FANG), Permian Resources (PR), Devon Energy (DVN), and landowners like Texas Pacific Land (TPL) and LandBridge (LB). Moreover, I like companies like WaterBridge (WBI) and Western Midstream (WES), which focus on bottlenecks like produced water and pipelines (egress).

There are obviously more picks, but these are some of my favorites for the years ahead.

Meanwhile, Canada has the benefit that its oil sands production has no decline rates. These are massive areas where oil is often produced in surface mines. These operations come with huge upfront costs, yet once they are operational, they allow producers to enjoy the benefits for decades with low breakeven prices. And while Canada has major takeaway problems like limited pipeline capacity, I believe these risks will fade over time, as the importance of Canada's supply will likely get a lot more emphasis.

In this area, I like the big guys, including Canadian Natural Resources (CNQ), which is a pure-play producer, and its peers, Suncor (SU) and Cenovus (CVE), which both have refining assets as well.

Right now, I'm watching all of these, prepared to buy on weakness, as I have little doubt they all have what it takes to generate substantial alpha for years to come.

Takeaway

My takeaway today is simple. Energy got crushed last year. This year, energy is "crushing it."

While my thesis looks good, that's not the point, as I'm a long-term investor. The Iran War may have accelerated the thesis, yet it has nothing to do with the core of why I'm bullish.

What matters to me is dwindling reserves, more proof of prudent capital allocation, a focus on shareholder returns, and fertile growth for economic growth broadening. Under these circumstances, I see long-term alpha in energy.

And given current headlines, I'm waiting for a bigger pullback in energy. The moment that happens, I happily buy the shares of people who think energy will go back to underperforming the market.

Risks To My Thesis

The biggest risk is a prolonged period of oil prices below $67/$65. That would hurt American oil producers, make new wells unprofitable, and potentially lead to lower output. While it's long-term bullish due to subdued supply growth, it's the factor that made energy such a tough place to be last year.

On top of that, because energy is cyclical, an increase in recession odds could hurt demand over time.

And, in general, most energy stocks are very volatile. Please do your own due diligence before putting any capital to work in this sector.

data.title: Gambling.com: More Risks Are Surfacing (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Thesis review

Gambling.com (GAMB) is one of the most underperforming names I have written on. Since my last article, where I rated it a strong buy, the stock has lost 64% of its value, and now it has an enterprise value of $240 million. This enterprise value is inclusive of the $160 million value of Odds Holdings, their largest acquisition. Meaning the rest of the business is roughly valued at $80 million.

As the title of my previous article entails, my main thesis was that, now that GAMB has transitioned to being a data provider instead of a marketing affiliate firm, the company has removed the headwind overhang associated with the secular decline of its legacy business due to Google SEO updates and AI disruption. The market has clearly priced in this secular decline, now that the acquired company is worth more than the legacy business. Even on the latest earnings call, management is more optimistic about growth expansion being realized in the subscription segment rather than in the marketing business.

Despite the drastic transition the firm is undertaking, the stock remains unattractive as momentum continues to be depressed. The Quant system has rated GAMB's momentum a D- given that YoY the stock has fallen by 71% and YTD it has fallen by 26.9%, the overall quant rating is a strong sell. Wall Street and Seeking Alpha analysts have maintained their strong buy ratings, stating that the market is still viewing the stock with the lens of its secularly declining business. New risks have surfaced for GAMB, making me wary of my initial rating; namely, the Swish lawsuit, the huge surge in debt and interest expenses, and the headwinds faced by sports betting as prediction markets become more pervasive. With the stated risks, I'm downgrading GAMB to a hold.

Legacy business is stagnating

The segment revenue charts below are illustrative of the business transition GAMB is undertaking. Performance marketing is in the last phase of the S-curve, while subscription revenue has recently spiked due to the inorganic growth from acquisitions. The advertising segment is growing decently; however, it remains the least contributor to overall revenue. When looking at total revenue, it's growing by double digits, masking the lack of organic growth.

In GAMB's case, organic growth mainly comes from casino revenue, as these are mostly the sites that pay for bettor leads. Compared to 2024, casino revenue grew 4.72%, from $92.2 million to $96.5 million, while sports revenue grew 172%, from $33.9 million to $65.09 million. Again, most of the growth from the sports segment was inorganic; in fact, organic revenue growth was -2% in 2025 vs. 2% in 2024 and 41% in 2023.

Now this means any hope of future topline growth in GAMB should be expected to come from the subscription business. We are not certain about the growth outlook of this segment, as the firm doesn't break down topline and EBITDA growth rates by individual business segments, e.g., OddsJam growth. One way to tell that OddsJam's growth is looking good is via the amendments to the acquisition deal. Just a quick recap on the original deal: GAMBS was expected to pay an additional $80 million as earnouts, contingent on Odds Holdings meeting performance targets, one of the targets being that the firm is expected to double 2024 EBITDA in 2026. The recent amendment is a tell that Odds Holdings is heading to hit those targets now that earnouts have converted to fixed payouts, with a balance estimated to be fully paid in April 2027, bringing the total acquisition value to $160 million when we exclude annual discounts.

Swish Lawsuit

My main issue with Odds Holdings is in regard to the ongoing lawsuit that was filed by Swish Analytics against the firm. The lawsuit claims that OddsJam scrapes modeled odds data from Swish's third-party clients like FanDuel and then resells it as its own. We mostly thought this case would be dismissed given that the data is public. Unfortunately, the case is still ongoing and could result in $100 million in damages to be paid if the case is lost. I find this risk to be material and should not be overlooked. Also, given that the case might be ongoing for longer than expected, we should expect legal expenses to weigh on margins going forward.

Financial Analysis

Q4 2025 Income Statement

In the latest quarter of 2025, GAMB increased revenue by 30% YoY to $46.24 million, which led to an estimate beating by $178,890.00. The growth was led by subscription revenue growing 440% to $11.82 million, followed by advertising growing 23% to $5.82 million and performance marketing growing 1% to $28.60 million. Cost of revenue increased to $6.89 million, resulting in gross profit increasing 19% to $39.34 million and landing margins at 85% vs. the sector margin of 53.93%. Operating expenses increased 117% to $64.87 million, caused by the restructuring expense of $32.50 million, mainly due to the earnout payment made to Odds Holdings shareholders as per the newly amended deal dictated. This led the firm to realize an operating loss of $25.53 million and a GAAP EPS of -$0.77, missing estimates by -$0.93. For Q1 of 2026, analysts estimate revenues of $40.25 million, implying a YoY decline of 1% and a QoQ decline of 13%. I find these estimates to be reasonable and in line with the firm's seasonal growth levels and the persistent decline of SEO-driven revenue.

FY2025 Income Statement

Full-year revenue also grew 30% to $165.45 million, with subscription revenue growing 382% to $41.13 million, advertising growing 17% to $20.75 million, and performance marketing growing 2% to $103.57 million. Restructuring costs amounted to $62.01 million, leading to operating expenses growing 117% to $181.98 million and operating losses growing 189% to $31.79 million. For the full year of 2026, management is guiding for revenue ranging between $170 million and $180 million, implying 8.79% YoY growth on the high-end. I find this guidance aggressive, implying that SEO declines are temporal, which, in my view, they actually look secular. During the earnings call, management hinted at a product release that will be revenue beneficial to the marketing business, which would explain the aggressive guide. I wouldn't want to take this at face value, especially now that the previous CEO has departed.

FY2025 Balance Sheet

Adding to the concerns regarding the stagnating growth of the legacy business is the highly leveraged GAMB's balance sheet. Cash increased 15% to $15.81 million, while current debt increased 199% to $10 million and long-term debt increased 458% to $108.62 million. If we don't see significant topline growth in the subsequent years, interest expenses will weigh heavily on earnings, leading to low free cash flow and a cash balance falling lower than current debt. We can see that most of the assets' value is trapped under intangibles; some of these are recently acquired assets that are not estimated to bring any medium-term cash flows; therefore, near-term debt coverage risks remain an overhang that can only be alleviated by free cash flow growth or stock issuance.

While performance marketing is stagnating and still accounting for 62% of overall revenue, we are also seeing interest expenses increasing, not good. Yes, earnings were negative in the last quarter due to restructuring costs; I wouldn't want to see them persisting to be negative for operational reasons. But this is where things are heading if this trend continues. The lowest level of interest expenses was seen in 2022 at $1.3 million, and has since increased 550% to $8.45 million in 2025.

Even though Odds Holdings' acquisition is pivotal for GAMB, it has surfaced many risks to the stocks that shouldn't be ignored. When I read a lot of articles from my fellow SA analysts, the common theme is that, as the stock continues to fall, management will opportunistically take advantage of this undervaluation to purchase more shares. In 2025 the firm has retired 672,000.00 worth of shares to the value of $5.6 million. The chart below clearly illustrates that the company has been purchasing fewer shares over the quarters, and the stock continues to tank. Now that the cash position is even more pressured by the debt levels, I don't see any aggressive buybacks in the coming quarters, meaning the stock price will remain at these levels if we don't see new buyers.

Bull Case

Even though I've switched bearish, GAMB remains a highly profitable firm trading at a discounted valuation. In fact, the firm exceeds all its industry peers on EBIT margins (21.37% vs. a sector margin of 11.10%) and most peers on FCF margins (16.81% vs 10%). And looking at the FCF chart below, we can see that free cash flow exceeded the $40 million mark on a trailing basis in the second half of 2025, the decline to $18.24 million in the last quarter was due to the Dec 2025 earnout payment to Odds Holdings shareholders. Management expects not just topline growth but also margin expansion from the data service business, if these expectations come to fruition, not only will GAMB be able to service its interest expenses, but it will also lower its debt burden and continue purchasing shares, more aggressively. I'll be paying more attention to FCF margin growth in subsequent quarters, as this is one indicator that could switch me to a bull again.

Conclusion

GAMB remains to be undervalued; a PE multiple of 9 vs. a sector median of 14.50 is definitely a steal. But with the risks that have recently surfaced, I think the discounted valuation is deserved until the dust settles. What I'm mainly looking forward to is the growth potential of the subscription business. Outside of the Swish lawsuit, one headwind for this business is the pricing pressure that might come from the enterprise side. As these sportsbooks begin to lose market share to prediction markets due to lack of vig, this will push them to cut costs on data expenses to maintain high margins. For these reasons I'm downgrading GAMB to a hold.

data.title: Reddit Starting To Look Straight-Up Attractive At This Price
data.description:
I covered Reddit (RDDT) back in February, assigning a buy rating (upgraded from hold put forward in July). Since then, the stock has been down +15%. At first glance, you’d assume that the stock is heading in the opposite direction of what I’d want, but I’m not so sure. I felt RDDT was quite expensive last time around, arguing, “I assign RDDT a buy rating, but it’s closer to hold territory than nearly all of my other buy recommendations.”

Yet Reddit’s tough start to 2026 has improved the valuation quite a bit, and the upside here is quite substantial. Hence, my update article. The valuation has changed enough to improve my outlook overall. I still wouldn’t call RDDT cheap, per se, but in a world of high PE ratios, it’s not outside of the norm either, in my opinion. As such, I’m keeping my buy rating and putting RDDT firmly in the middle of my buy recommendations rather than at the trailing edge.

Reddit Remains One Of The More Human-Credible Websites Out There

Years back, I used to do a fair bit of SEO writing. As with many an industry, seeing how the sausage is made can dampen one’s appetite. While I typically went out of my way to ensure that I was writing accurate information and otherwise adding value to readers, keyword and content stuffing was for many the norm. Unfortunately, low-grade content leads to a low-grade World Wide Web. People complain about AI slop now, but really, the previous iteration of the Internet could be viewed as SEO slop.

Reddit, however, has by and large been an oasis in terms of genuine, authentic content. Make no mistake, you’ll find spammers and stuffers on Reddit, along with bots, AI comments, etc. By and large, however, the Reddit community is pretty good at sniffing such content out (although I’m sure some junk content does go undetected).

As AI gains prominence and AI-generated content proliferates, Reddit likely will still remain one of the better sources of human-generated content. The ability to niche down on the website is especially useful. There are plenty of specific topical sub-Reddits to explore, including things like hiking, taking care of dogs, and removing earwax (yes, there is actually a rather busy sub-Reddit that focuses on earwax removal.)

If Reddit can keep its website relatively bot- and AI-free (big if), the site will continue to be a major value to everyday people, AI developers (who often buy access to Reddit’s data), and advertisers, among others. Of course, there are no guarantees here, but Reddit is still one of the better bets in terms of human-generated content and engagement.

Reddit is also one of the better sources of fresh data for AI developers to tap into. I covered this a bit more in my prior article on Reddit, but essentially, AI developers are running out of sources of fresh data. The more data a developer has access to, the more they can train their models. The more they train their models, the more powerful they become. Now, with developers running out of fresh data, there’s a risk that it’ll slow progress.

Anyway, Reddit has begun to monetize selling data to AI developers, with the larger contracts worth many tens of millions. Keep in mind, the content itself is largely user-generated, which should help Reddit keep costs low and margins high.

RDDT Fundamentals And Valuation Considerations

RDDT still isn’t cheap, per se, with a TTM GAAP PE ratio of about ~48. The non-GAAP is more attractive at ~28, and the forward non-GAAP looks even better at ~19. The primary difference in these two numbers is Stock-Based Compensation, which is illustrated below. While SBC does dilute shareholder value, RDDT is still producing growth that you’d expect with a hot technology startup. These numbers mark a solid improvement from when I last looked at RDDT, when it had 33.12 non-GAAP PE TTM and 57.30 GAAP PE TTM ratios.

Reddit SBC

YOY revenue growth weighs in at just over 69%. This is expected to slow to 46.5% revenue growth going forward, but that’s still blistering growth. Meanwhile, the strong Gross Profit and Net Income Margins (TTM) reflect a mature, profitable company, and are attractive even by the standards of the tech industry and the typically high profitability it enjoys. Someday, growth will taper off, but that’s likely not going to happen for a good long while.

Reddit’s balance sheet is also healthy, with the company carrying only $310 million in total liabilities, versus ~2.477 billion in cash and short-term assets and ~$590 million in accounts receivable. So while Reddit is enjoying hot startup-like growth, it has a balance sheet that you’d expect from a mature, profitable company.

I actually initiated Reddit as a hold, primarily because I am worried about advertising-dependent websites right now. I still am wary of advertising-dependent companies, and that is something I’d keep an eye on. If growth were to drop to, say, 20-30% YOY, I’d likely start to get nervous.

My fellow Seeking Alpha analysts, in aggregate (which may include my prior buy recommendation from February), rate it a buy. Wall Street also comes away with a buy rating. The quant tools are a bit more skeptical, it seems, with a hold rating. Valuation gets a poor mark at D-, and I do agree this is still a pretty pricey stock, but I believe the growth and profitability justify the high valuation. If RDDT continues to slide, I’ll likely view that as an opportunity to add more on the cheap.

Why I Could Be Wrong

RDDT is still pretty pricey as stocks go, and I may simply be overestimating its potential. AI presents opportunities, but also threats, including the risk of the site being drowned out via AI-generated content and engagement. Reddit might also simply go out of style. It’s not uncommon to find people complaining about moderators, company policies, etc. Someday, negative sentiments may fester and spread, and the audience might move on.

Key Takeaway: Reddit Runs With The Pack

After a rather tepid buy recommendation in February, I’m upgrading RDDT in the middle of my to-buy pack. This is a solid investment given the high upside. The risks are considerable but not enough to scare away investing, at least not for me. This is a stock I'd keep an eye on, with growth being perhaps the most crucial barometer for now.

data.title: Big BDC Dividend Yields: 1 Golden Opportunity And 1 Value Trap
data.description:
The business development company (BIZD) sector has been selling off recently as private credit has come under increasing scrutiny and has suffered from numerous negative media headlines recently. As a result, numerous popular publicly traded BDCs now trade at large discounts to net asset value as well as their own historical averages, presenting potentially compelling buying opportunities for income-focused and long-term-oriented value investors who are willing to be patient during the current negativity.

However, not all BDCs are created equal, with some having legitimately high risk. I think that at least somewhat validates the discounts they are currently experiencing.

With that in mind, in this article, I'm going to compare two of these opportunities and share which one I think is worth buying right now and which one I'm steering clear of.

Key Risks Facing The BDC Sector

Before I get into the details of these two BDCs, the biggest risks facing BDCs right now:

  • The Fed has been on a path towards cutting interest rates, which tends to weigh on BDCs, since most of their investments are in floating-rate loans. Whenever the Fed cuts rates, their net investment income tends to decline. This has already led several BDCs to cut their dividends.

  • Additionally, many BDCs have fixed-rate debt maturing in the near term, which they are having to refinance at materially higher interest rates than they were underwritten at, as much of this debt was taken on about five years ago following COVID-19, when the Fed slashed rates aggressively. Today, while the Fed is in the process of cutting rates, long-term interest rates have risen significantly over the past half-decade, especially after oil prices skyrocketed following the war in Iran.

  • Moreover, there is growing concern about the health of the BDC sector, as capital has flooded into the space in recent years at a much faster clip than new loans have become available. This has created a supply-demand imbalance, which has led some to fear that underwriting standards have slipped, and has also seen spread compression leading to less attractive returns with potentially increased risks.

  • Additionally, the war in Iran and the disruption to oil prices are leading many to have concerns that we could be on the cusp of a material recession, which would likely lead to a spike in non-accruals and even defaults, which would mean lower income and lower NAV for most BDCs.

  • Last but not least, there is concern that portfolios that have considerable exposure to software loans could be disrupted by AI.

A Shrinking Ice Cube With Troubling Fundamentals

The first BDC I'm going to discuss is FS KKR Capital Corp (FSK). It is a large BDC with a roughly $13 billion portfolio diversified across 232 companies, and its portfolio is not that defensively positioned, with only about 57.8% of it allocated toward first-lien senior secured loans, well below the BDC peer average. Additionally, its underwriting performance has been fairly lackluster, with non-accruals at 5.5% at cost and 3.4% on a fair-value basis, which is among the highest in the BDC sector. Moreover, non-income-producing investments comprise a whopping 9.5% of fair value. Furthermore, software services make up about 16.4% of its portfolio, which is a sector that is considered riskier in the current AI disruption environment. As a result, FSK was recently downgraded by Moody's to junk from investment grade.

The balance sheet is in okay shape, with net debt to equity at 1.22 times, which is below its 1.25 times upper limit on its target leverage range, though it does not have much flexibility there at all. Meanwhile, FSK recently had to refinance $1 billion worth of fixed-rate loans that were at a 3.4% interest rate that matured in 2026, which means that its new debt will be pressuring its net investment income moving forward, including in Q1. Additionally, 90% of its liabilities mature in 2027, which keeps this near-term maturity risk somewhat limited, other than its January 2026 refinancing that it recently had to do. Its liquidity of $3.8 billion allows it some wiggle room to handle potential issues.

The Biggest Indictment Against FSK

Perhaps the biggest indictment against FSK, though, is its consistent and substantial NAV erosion. For example, over the past year alone, its NAV per share has declined from $22.75 per share, or 11.6% of its NAV in a single year, which effectively wipes out the dividend it paid out over that period of time. This past quarter alone saw a $0.89 per share decline from realized and unrealized losses. This paints a picture of a very unsustainable income machine that is a shrinking ice cube, even though its dividend yield is attractive.

This just showed up in the company's recent dividend cut from 70 cents per share in Q4 to 48 cents per share in Q1, which includes a 45-cent per share base dividend and a 3-cent per share supplemental dividend. Perhaps the most jarring was how analysts pressed management on the earnings call about the prospects of buybacks, with the price-to-NAV ratio showing a massive discount to book value. However, management was non-committal, which signals that its alignment with shareholders is questionable at best. This stands out in stark contrast to Blue Owl Capital Corporation (OBDC), Blackstone Secured Lending (BXSL), and several others, which have bought back stock aggressively whenever it trades at a discount to NAV. Meanwhile, FSK's fee structure, with a 1.5% base management fee and a 17.5% incentive fee, is not particularly attractive relative to that of peers. Their refusal to consider cutting the fee following their poor performance stands out as a negative contrast to other BDCs like Oaktree Specialty Lending (OCSL) and Goldman Sachs BDC (GSBD), which have slashed their fees after poor performance.

Thus, while the discount to NAV is incredible at 51% presently and the dividend yield looks very attractive as well at 17.7%, the very poor underlying quality of the management team and its underwriting track record, as well as their recent downgrade to junk credit rating, signal to me that this is a very low-quality BDC. Even if its current discount to NAV is significantly overstated, there are other much higher-quality BDCs trading at deep discounts where the alignment with shareholders is much stronger, and that is therefore where I would much rather place my bets in a sector that is already besieged with uncertainty and headwinds.

The Blue-Chip BDC Built To Weather the Storm

The other BDC I'm going to discuss today is Golub Capital BDC (GBDC), which I think is a much more worthwhile investment right now. Like FSK, GBDC has a fairly large portfolio, with $8.6 billion worth of assets spread across 420 portfolio companies, with an average position size of just 0.2%, which makes it much more diversified than FSK. Additionally, GBDC is much more defensively positioned, with 92% of the portfolio invested in first-lien loans, which is much higher than the BDC average of 80% and FSK's 57.8%. Furthermore, its non-accruals are well below the sector average, just 0.8% on a fair-value basis and 1.3% at cost. Additionally, its underlying portfolio is performing well, with 89% of it rated 4 or 5 on a scale of 1 to 5, indicating a fairly small watch list. While its software exposure is very large at 27%, the company has 20 years and over a thousand software deals' worth of experience, with only five defaults, which amount to just 0.25% of the total $145 billion committed to software investments.

Moreover, management signaled a prudent and cautious approach to the sector that should serve it well through these uncertain times. Additionally, the credit ratings clearly see a high-quality business with a strong balance sheet, as Moody's awards GBDC a Baa2 stable credit rating, which is among the highest in the sector and two notches ahead of FSK's. That said, its leverage ratio of 1.23 times puts it on par with FSK's and in a slight and very small wiggle room below its upper target limit; however, the company's underwriting strength more than makes up for this.

Why GBDC's Long-Term Track Record Seals the Deal

Additionally, GBDC's NAV per share has been much more stable than FSK's, with only a very slight sequential decline this past quarter. Additionally, since its 2010 IPO, GBDC has generated a 9.5% annualized IRR on its NAV, which has outperformed a lot of its BDC peers and high-yield debt (HYG) sectors while delivering equity-like annualized total returns over that period. In fact, GBDC has generated a net gain rate since inception of 0.06% annualized, versus a peer average loss rate of negative 0.86%, reflecting its very strong underwriting and recovery capabilities.

Additionally, its dividend of 33 cents per share is well covered by NII, as this past quarter delivered 38 cents per share of adjusted NII, giving it about a 15% coverage rate on the base dividend. GBDC also plans to pay out about 50% of adjusted NII above its 33-cent-per-share base dividend, which gives it further upside as it finds ways to raise rates, including through interest rate hikes, while also leaving some capital behind to help sustain NAV and engage in buybacks. In fact, GBDC has been repurchasing shares actively, including in Q4 at an 8% discount to NAV, and today, with the NAV per share even higher at over 15%, buybacks are likely still active and even more accretive, especially with their retained cash after the dividend. Insiders are also well aligned, with 8% insider ownership, and with a 10.5% dividend yield, even before considering supplementals, GBDC's dividend looks sustainable and attractive.

The Rare BDC I'm Buying While Others Run for the Exits

While GBDC is not nearly as cheap as FSK, the insider alignment, the underwriting quality, the credit rating, and the company's long-term track record are vastly superior. Therefore, I view GBDC as an attractive "Buy" on the recent dip in the BDC sector, while I am steering clear of FSK, even if it is ridiculously cheap. While GBDC is a small position in our portfolio, there are several other blue-chip, deeply undervalued BDCs with sustainable and attractive dividend yields, with strong insider alignment and stock buybacks. We are slowly but surely adding to our portfolio that yields about 8%, with below-market beta and long-term total return outperformance, at High Yield Investor.
